mnemonics.title: Decision Tree Sorting Bound
mnemonics.content: STILL: S(Tirling's) = n log n - 1.44n, T(ree) must have n! leaves, I(nformation) = log₂(n!), L(ower bound) = Ω(n log n), L(ower bound) is tight for optimal sorts like Merge Sort.

mnemonics.title: Binary Search Tree Height
mnemonics.content: SEARCH: S(orted array) → E(very level halves) → A(nswers): ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉ comparisons, R(ight or left) → C(hoose branch) → H(eight) = log₂(n+1).

mnemonics.title: Decision Tree Properties
mnemonics.content: LEAVES: L(ast) = n! for sorting, E(very comparison) = 1 bit info, A(ny binary tree) ≤ 2^h leaves, V(erify) with Stirling, E(stimate) height: log₂(n!) ≈ n log₂ n - 1.44n.

acronyms.expansion: Decision Tree - binary tree model for comparison algorithms
acronyms.usage: Used to prove lower bounds by analyzing the minimum height required to distinguish all possible outcomes

acronyms.expansion: Information-Theoretic Lower Bound - minimum comparisons needed based on information theory
acronyms.usage: log₂(number of possible outcomes) comparisons needed to distinguish all cases

acronyms.expansion: Comparison-Based Sorting - algorithms that only use element comparisons
acronyms.usage: Subject to Ω(n log n) lower bound; examples: merge sort, heap sort, quick sort

visualTips.visualization: Draw a binary tree where each internal node is a comparison (like a ≤ b), left branch = yes, right branch = no. Leaves at bottom represent final answers. The deeper the leaf, the more comparisons needed.

Think of n! permutations as numbered doors. Each comparison gives 1 bit (2 choices). Need enough bits to identify which door: log₂(n!) comparisons. Draw a tree branching 2-way repeatedly until reaching n! leaves.
Imagine n! as a pyramid with base n and height n. Taking log₂ compresses it to n log₂ n shape, minus a triangular correction 1.44n representing the square root factor.
Compare a balanced BST to a skewed tree. Balanced = minimum height = log₂(n+1), optimal. Skewed = height n, worst case. Draw both side-by-side.

analogies.analogy: Like a tournament bracket where each match (comparison) eliminates half the possibilities. After log₂(n!) matches, you find the winner (sorted order).
Each comparison is like a yes/no question that cuts the search space in half. It's like 20 questions - each answer halves the possibilities.
Imagine n! different locked boxes, each containing one possible way to arrange items. Each comparison test gives you a clue to eliminate half the boxes until only one remains - the sorted order.

The decision tree lower bound is like a speed limit - no matter how good your car (algorithm) is, you cannot go faster than the limit (n log n for sorting). Non-comparison sorts are like finding a shortcut that's not on the main road.
